article.title: NASA's Artemis II Lifts Off Today, Sending Four Astronauts Around the Moon for the First Time in Over 50 Years
article.summary: The SLS rocket is set to launch at 6:24 p.m. EDT from Kennedy Space Center, carrying the first crew to travel beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Space Launch System rocket is scheduled to lift off from Kennedy Space Center at 6:24 p.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 1, carrying four astronauts on a 10-day journey around the Moon and back. The Artemis II mission will mark the first time humans have traveled beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Weather forecasters are giving the launch an 80 percent chance of favorable conditions, with cumulus clouds, ground winds, and solar weather identified as the primary concerns, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/).

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028) on NASA's restructuring of the broader Artemis program, which pushed the first crewed lunar landing to 2028.

## The Crew

The four-person crew represents several historic firsts. Commander Reid Wiseman, a former Navy fighter pilot who spent 165 days aboard the International Space Station in 2014, will lead the mission. Pilot Victor Glover, a U.S. Navy aviator, becomes the first Black astronaut assigned to a lunar mission, as reported by [Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch).

Mission specialist Christina Koch holds the record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days and participated in the first all-female spacewalk, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen, a former Royal Canadian Air Force fighter pilot, rounds out the crew as the first Canadian set to travel to the Moon, as noted by [Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch).

## What We Know

The SLS rocket stands 322 feet tall and will generate over 8.8 million pounds of thrust at liftoff, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). The crew will ride inside the Orion capsule, which NASA has named "Integrity," as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

The mission profile calls for a 90-minute low-Earth orbit insertion, followed by a 24-hour high-altitude orbit at 44,555 miles for systems testing. A trans-lunar injection burn approximately 25 hours after launch will send the crew on a four-day coast to the Moon, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). The spacecraft will pass within 4,100 miles of the lunar surface before using a free-return trajectory around the far side of the Moon.

At its farthest point, Orion will travel 252,000 miles from Earth, surpassing Apollo 13's record distance of 248,655 miles, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). The total journey will cover approximately 685,000 miles over 10 days, with splashdown expected in the Pacific Ocean on April 10, as reported by [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1).

The crew will also set a reentry speed record, hitting approximately 25,000 mph as they return to Earth — surpassing Apollo 10's 1969 mark, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Orion's 16.5-foot-wide heat shield will bear the full force of that reentry, as noted by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## Previous Delays

The mission was originally scheduled for early February 2026 but was postponed after a liquid hydrogen leak was detected during a practice countdown. A separate helium flow issue in the rocket's upper stage was subsequently discovered and resolved, as reported by [Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch). NASA confirmed that all technical anomalies have been addressed and that the hardware is in launch-ready configuration as of March 27, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/).

## What We Don't Know

While Artemis II will validate critical systems, the crew will not land on the Moon. The mission serves as a precursor to Artemis IV, now targeted for 2028 as the first crewed lunar surface landing under the restructured program timeline. Whether the data gathered during this flyby will reveal unexpected challenges for future surface missions remains to be seen.

The launch window extends through April 6, with two-hour windows available each day after sunset, should today's attempt be scrubbed due to weather or technical issues, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Commander Wiseman captured the moment's weight in a preflight statement: "I think the nation and the world has been waiting a long time to do this again," as quoted by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).
